posts.name: Brooks Haley
posts.cooked: I’m looking at doing a magnum or two but can’t decide which direction I want to go. Dinner will be some sort of beef dish, so I am thinking some Brunello or Barolo. Also, where have you guys had the best luck buying older vintage bottles? I always like to open those on holidays. Everyone seems to get excited over them.

posts.name: Sam Chung
posts.cooked:
I will be starting off with Cedric Bouchard Urseles '13 that has nice delicate bubbles, into PYCM Corton-Charlemagne '14 which will be big, and finish off with Beaucastel '89. If everyone is alive by then, I’ll probably open the Moulin Touchais '68.

I just hope the Beaucastel is not corked… If it is, I’m thinking Clape Cornas '13 or Rougeard Les Clos '14.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked: Egly Ouriet Brut Grand Cru, then 2008 Dom Rose. My dad will probably open a red wine.

posts.name: Kevin Santana
posts.cooked:
'89 Ch. La Fleur de Gay
'20 Salty Goats Oakville Cab Sauv.

Menu has not been finalized, but these are a few I’m eager to open and try.

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked:
My family celebrates Christmas and I have my Christmas Eve lineup planned ahead. We don’t have a lot of wine aficionados, but we do have a number of people who enjoy good wine. I also don’t have the bank account to bring any Petrus, La Tache, etc. I am keeping it fairly simple and straightforward and hopefully bringing something everyone will like. Here is the lineup I will be bringing (I am hosting):

2016 Groth Cabernet Sauvignon
2019 Aubert Pinot Noir UV-SL
2019 Aubert Chardonnay CIX
2005 Joh Jos Prum WS Spatlese
2005 Joh Jos Prum WS Auslese

Most of us are Pinot and Riesling drinkers, but my aunt and uncle are big Groth fans so I will bring a botte of that as well. It’s not my favorite, but tis the season for giving. Others will be brining wine and most will probably bring a Cabernet so I thought I would go with less Cab.

Edit- I should also say most of the guests drink almost 100% US based wines so I try to keep it basic for them

For Christmas day evening I haven’t thought about it much but I assume we will crack another Aubert Pinot and JJ Prum bottle. I love to see what others are planning. There are several others who have shared in a topic called “holiday lineup” for Christmas Eve from a couple of days ago as well if you want to see those.

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked: It is always exciting to bring an older bottle. For people that don’t drink wine often they always love it and they also get to have a new wine experience. With luck we may create some new Berserkers

posts.name: Bri@n Kelly
posts.cooked:
It may be just my wife and me this year, with me being the only one drinking wine, so very limited. But probably two or three from this list:

2005 Schramsberg J Schram
2010 Billecart Salmon Rose
2012 Bolly LGA
2015 Pierre Peters Chetillons
2015 Bouchard Corton Charlemagne
2002 Diamond Creek Volcanic Hill
2013 Viader
2017 Bella Oaks
2005 Suduiraut

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked: With that list you can throw darts and you will be happy.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: The last bottle of Piper Rare 2002 and a magnum of Domaine de Chevalier 1966.

posts.name: Amanda Hay
posts.cooked:
I’m unreasonably excited for Christmas dinner. It’s just my partner and me. Since it’s my first year collecting wine, I’ve found Winebid to be really reliable for getting older bottles.

I’ll be making roast goose for the first time, with local beef bourguignon pot pie to enjoy that week as well. So some bottles from the following list:

2011 Le Brun Servenay BdB
2009 Beaufort Polisy BdN

1999 Kalin Cellars Pinot Noir
2009 Louis Jadot Clos de Ursules

2003 The Standish Shiraz

2021 Extradimensional Wine Co Mariana’s Cabernet Sauvignon (already coravin’d this and it was delicious)
2016 or 2017 Roy Piper

2014 Suduiraut

posts.name: Paul @bbott
posts.cooked: 2009 Haut Batailley and 2009 Potensac are standing up at my BIL’S, not sure what they are planning for food, but they should be a good starting point.

posts.name: Kevin Santana
posts.cooked: I had the '17 Bella Oaks earlier in the year, and it was stunning! This producer is definitely one of my favorites from Napa. You also cannot go wrong with the '02 DC Volcanic Hill.

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked: Thinking Roederer or Vazart-Coquart and a TBD Puffeney red on Christmas Day at home. Also feeling N. Rhone and Loire CF this holiday season with extended family.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
For our Danish Christmas dinner you need either something with a bit of fruit punch and alcohol or sweet white with a bit of age on it (Spätlese probably the most popular choice).

I will open a Chateau des Tours, CDR, 2016 and a Huet, Clos du Bourg, Demi-Sec, 2014.

Maybe a Champagne while cooking dinner.

posts.name: Sam Chung
posts.cooked: Had the 2016 Chateau des Tours last month! Such a pretty wine. Hope you enjoy!

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked: Ok so where is Christmas this year? I forgot when you told me the address

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Dinner will be my mom’s lobster crumble, so we’ll be having Champagne and Ramey Chardonnay.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked: Christmas at the in-laws this year in Bretagne. My father-in-law has been battling health issues so he’ll probably ask me to go downstairs and pick the wines. I will come back with whatever looks good, can be retrieved from his 3000+ bottle tetris pile of wine within an acceptable timeframe and without breaking too many bottles. If memory serves me right, I cleared a path to some Chambolle and NSG last time I was there…
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked: Wow, I never knew this existed. This checks all of the boxes for me!

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked:
Have the 2 Ravenswood merlot mags that will get opened during the course of the week. I may grab a Neal magnum if I have one at my offsite.

Will probably open a Spottswoode 14’ to see where that is at. Need to get into my locker to bring some things home.

Will also need a nice white since we typically do king crab legs for Xmas eve.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: The food? It’s ridiculously easy to make.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
sam_chung:

Had the 2016 Chateau des Tours last month! Such a pretty wine. Hope you enjoy!

I will 100% enjoy it. The CDR Has become my go to wine for Christmas. Got four 2016’s left. After that I am not sure if I will buy at current prices or move to Eben Sadie’s Soldaat

posts.name: Nick G A J E W S K I
posts.cooked:
Lerner Project Armstrong Ranch
Arnyca Cab Franc
Memento Mori Vine Hill Ranch
Laurent-Perrier Grand Siecle
posts.display_username: Nick G A J E W S K I

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: I have a 1989 Beaucastel for holidays too…may we both be TCA-free for Xmas 23.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: Sheesh…I’d go BBD, Bolly, Bouchard, Diamond Creek.

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked:
Good question.

I always make my native Swedish Xmas dishes, so brined ham in oven with a bread crust, pickled herring, meatballs and a Janssons Temptation of course. None of it very easy to pair with food, in my experience. It really is snaps and beer kind of food. But we can’t have beer being a winemaker for chrissakes (the snaps - no problem)!

So probably will try to get my hand on some sort of nice Riesling that has perception of sweetness, a style I myself don’t make. Any suggestions for a good German trocken that has just a hint of this to pair with this rather heavy food? It needs to cut through the grease with lots of acid, but mustn’t be too austere (as I don’t think it will go with the flavors).

link_counts.title: Best Janssons Frestelse Recipe - How to Make Jansson’s Temptation

posts.name: David Lewin
posts.cooked: We too will be drinking our last bottle of 2002 Piper Rare.

posts.name: John Preston
posts.cooked:
I am leaning towards birth year wines since our kids will be in attendance…'97 Dunn HM, '01 Togni. Others TBD.

Cheers,
JP

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: Neither mine nor my wife’s extended family are big wine drinkers but they still expect me to bring wine. This is a good time to open stuff I like that won’t break the bank like Collection 242 and Bedrock.

posts.name: H 3n ry  Br 1m 0
posts.cooked: We celebrate the night before but I have Louis Roederer 243 and Suduiraut 2014 lined up for a casual Christmas brunch.

Thinking a 242 or ‘13 or ‘14 Roederer brut, a vintage tbd Roederer rosé, Charles Heidsieck rosé “special edition” and then match some crowd pleasing reds to the food. With the anticipated chaos (small kids, multiple families) this is the time for wines that are good but not “the event” so 242 and the CH rosé just became frontrunners.

de Négoce Lot 68 2018 Sonoma Cabernet Sauvignon. Paid $12, technically worth $80. Delicious juice. I love the QPR word going down through my throat and into my veins…
Merry Christmas!

posts.name: Eric Johnson
posts.cooked:
I’m having an extended celebration this year since my family will all be together for the first time in ages. I have 3 dinners for roughly 10-12 per night.

Dec 23rd - King Crab, Oysters, and Risotto

2002 Ruinart Rose (if I win the auction)
1995 Verget Chassange Montrachet 1er Cru La Romanee
2015 J.J. Prum Wehlener Sonnenuhr Kabinett

2019 Riverain Pinot Noir Silver Eagle (Magnum)

Dec 25th - Morgan Ranch Standing Rib Roast and Halibut

2011 Louis Roederer Champagne Vintage Brut Rosé (Magnum)
2000 Pavie Macquin or 2003 Pavie
2014 Verite le Desir
2014 Kobayashi Cab Franc
2016 Riverain Reserve Cabernet

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.cooked:
Fewer people this year calls for fewer bottles… As Lasse hinted at the Danish Christmas dinner is centered in a very fatty food intake and so several palate cleansers are needed to not feel totally terrible at the end of the night.

Bichery - La Source to start the orgy
La Ferme de la Sansonniere - Fouchardes 2012
Chateau du Hureau - Saumur Champigny 2002
Jocelyn et Yves Lafoy - Cote Rotie 1997
— Sure my dad will find something in his cellar too.
Finally while observing others gob up rice pudding in our traditional game for a lone piece of almond, I’ll eat cheese and with that I’ll drink;
Patrick Corbineau - Oxydatif Cab Franc 2003 or La Cave Reine Jeanne 2000 Chardy

posts.name: Amanda Hay
posts.cooked: Ha, not all in one day! We’ll see how we do over the week…

reply_to_user.name: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.cooked:
cellartracker.com

NV Charles Heidsieck Champagne Rosé Special Edition, France, Champagne -...

Average of 91.7 points in 14 community wine reviews on NV Charles Heidsieck Champagne Rosé Special Edition, plus professional notes, label images, wine details, and recommendations on when to drink.

It’s an interesting wine - it’s either based on 2005 or entirely 2005, and disgorged in 2019. I believe it was only released in NY to commemorate “Champagne Charlie’s” first to nyc. There’s the crowd pleasing rosé elements combined with mature notes, but bottle variation has been quite high.

link_counts.title: NV Charles Heidsieck Champagne Rosé Special Edition, France, Champagne - CellarTracker

posts.name: Jonathan Dorris
posts.cooked:
This may be my last year of drinking wine so I hope to go big. I bought a 2006 Cheval Blanc for probably my one and only taste of the producer that I plan on opening.

As for where to buy back vintages, I use two retailers: www.thatcherswine.com and www.europeanwineresource.com

Just the other day I made a post about how much I really appreciate Thatcher’s service and I’ve had similarly great service from EWR folks. Oh if you buy from @ThatcherBakerBriggs then use his/their “WBERK5” promo code for 5% off just for wine berserkers like yourself.

Happy holidays to all. Take care of yourself and each other

posts.name: Jon Cronin
posts.cooked: I’m aiming for beef roast dinner just before the 49er game that night, sides and snacks along the way. So, I’m thinking of Les Fiefs de Lagrange Saint Julien Bordeaux, their 2nd label, around $40 in my area. Nothing splashy and exorbitant, but solid. Thanksgiving 2019, I had their 2014 vintage which was singing nicely.

posts.name: Ben Bader

  • Mariana’s Vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon

My family doesn’t have many drinkers in it, so two bottles will do nicely.

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked:
First time we will be hosting Christmas, and since it is in Vienna with the family all travelling from Denmark, it will be several days of feast and drinking. Haven’t decided on what will be drunk on which days, but have gotten the following wine shipped from Danish off-site, and am sure they will all be consumed… And yes it is a lot of wine - but we are big group and it is across several days =)

White Burgundy:

  • Henri Germain Meursault 20
  • Simon Collin Chassagne 21
  • Bruno Lorenzon Mercurrey 1er Champs Martin Blanc 2020
  • Bruno Lorenzon Mercurrey 1er Piece 15 2019
  • Guffens Heynen Macon-Pierreclos ‘Juliette et les vielles de Chavigne’ 2018
  • Guffens Heynen Pouilly Fuisse 1. Jus des Hauts des Vignes 2018
  • Guffens Heynen Macon En Crazy 2021
  • Guffens Heynen Macon Deuxieme de Chavigne 2021
  • Desjourneys Puilly-Fuisse Vignes De La Cote 2019
  • Desjourneys Puilly-Fuisse Vignes Blanches 2019

Red Burgundy:

  • Soyard Hautes Cote de Nuits 2020
  • Soyard Hermaion 2021
  • Bruno Lorenzon Mercurrey 1er Piece 13 2020
  • Soyard Cras Monopole Rouge 2020
  • G. Roumier Chambolle-Musigny 2018
  • Mugneret Gibourg Bourgogne Rouge 2012 and 2017
  • Arnaud Mortet Gevrey Ma Cuvee 2020 in MG
  • Denis Mortet Gevrey Mes Cinq Terroirs 2021

Other Reds:

  • Des Tours Vacqueyras 2011
  • G. Burlotto Barolo ‘Aclivi’ 2018
  • Conterno-Nervi Gattinara 2018
  • Tours Cote Du Rhone 2018

posts.name: Brian Tuite  GOML!
posts.cooked: Open that Salty Goats couple days ahead and let it breathe. You’ll thank me later.

user_status.description: Wine doesn’t have to be tertiary to be enjoyable

posts.name: Kevin Santana
posts.cooked: Thanks for the advice! Appreciate it

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
Like @G_Curd said, what’s your address again? As a true son of Maine, lobster for Christmas sounds amazing!

At our place, likely going to be a grilled version of Julia Child’s Dijon-garlic leg of lamb with Burgundy. Likely open with a white Burg and then shirt to a red: Fourrier, Berthaut-Gerbet or a Vosne Beaux-Monts.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Steve_McL:

As a true son of Maine, lobster for Christmas sounds amazing!

Many times a year while in Montreal we would do “Guédilles au homard” with Louis Michel Chablis. Good times. It’s basically a lobster roll. We would get them for 7 CAD a pound (4.50 USD). Many lobsters would die. Many grapes would be sacrificed. Many people would leave happy.
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: T 0 m Altmayer
posts.cooked: Likely an ‘00 Taittinger Comtes to start and a magnum of ‘19 A Tribute to Grace Shake Ridge Ranch.

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked: Some of these meals just sound truly amazing. I am jealous of some of the Christmas//Holiday meals and wines I will be missing. Family and fellowship is the most important part of the holiday for me, but my gosh some of these pairings might make me take a year off from family to travel and have Christmas with some berserkers .

posts.name: RAUL PUGA
posts.cooked:
I will be roasting a 60lb pig “Cuban style” in a Caja China on our traditional Christmas Eve celebration, Nochebuena. It’s a full day affair so I will start with some bubbles. Probably Billecart just not sure yet. Trying to figure out the rest of the lineup. I usually go with Spanish Riojas but I want to try something new. Any suggestions would be appreciated.

Happy Holidays!!

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Well, I’m gonna be miles away from Rioja but I’ve done suckling pig (in the oven) 4 or 5 Christmas in the last 15 years and I’ve really enjoyed:

  • Moulin-à-Vent
  • Austrian Blaufränkisch
  • Côte-Rôtie
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: @ s t r 1 d    k e e l
posts.cooked:
We used to go back East for the holidays, but since the pandemic, family dynamics have changed, and we have stayed in SoCal. It will be just us two and my dog for a third year in a row.
I’m debating the food menu. I want to make duck, but my husband is not a fan, so it will most likely be beef.

Food:
starter: caviar or smoked salmon (Most likely the latter, since I have yet to get organized and order anything).
Salad with little gem lettuce and whatever looks fresh
Main: ribeye roast sous vide & reverse sear with red wine pan sauce, fingerling potatoes and string beans (if the string beans look fresh - otherwise some other vegetable)
dessert: Probably poached pears in crème anglaise.

Wines:
Chavost Eureka or 2004 Ployez Jacquemart Liesse d’Harbonville
2011 Domaine de la Janasse CdP Vielles Vignes (opening this more for my husband than me. I strongly disliked my prior three bottles of this) or, if I can find the bottle 2012 Conti Costanti Brunello. I’ll probably drink champagne with the roast.
2003 La Tour Blanche Sauternes. I know it’s heresy, but LTB is my favorite sauternes.
posts.display_username: @ s t r 1 d    k e e l

posts.cooked: Opening a first bottle of Kinsman Eades cab from the stash ('16 Anjea) and maybe the received today Kobayashi Sans Souf. Christmas day, a mag of 2014 Beckman “Red” (mostly syrah, tiny bit of grenache).
